title: Why Your Children's Tantrums Trigger Your Rage
dek: Your child’s shriek hits your eardrums like hot needles. Your shoulders tighten, your breath stops, and a red wash of anger floods your chest. This fury feels like failure, but it’s older than discipline. It’s your body recalling wounds you didn’t know you had. The tantrum isn’t the problem—the echoes are. Here’s how to listen instead of lashing out.
bodyMarkdown:
You are standing in the frozen-food aisle, the fluorescent light needling your temple, and your three-year-old has just gone liquid. Her back arches, her heels drum the linoleum, and the sound that pours from her open mouth is not a cry—it’s a sonic drill. You feel it in your teeth first, then your gut. Your palms sweat. A stranger glances over, and a primitive part of your brain reads that glance as judgment, as threat. Your hand clamps around her arm harder than you meant to. You hiss something through a jaw so tight your molars ache. Later, you will sit in the driver’s seat with your forehead on the steering wheel, hating yourself. You will tell your partner you have a “short fuse.” You will google anger management techniques at 2 a.m.

But the question you keep dodging is this: Whose rage are you actually regurgitating?

### The Body Remembers What You’ve Buried

You were taught that anger is the problem. That if you could just breathe deeper, count to ten, or tap your wrist, you would stop morphing into a person you don’t recognize. This is the wellness-blog lie you’ve been served for years, and it keeps you stuck because it skips the body entirely. The body remembers. It has kept every score you never settled, every threat you froze through, every time your small hands balled into fists and were told to unclench. When your child’s tantrum detonates, it does not land in your prefrontal cortex, the seat of rational thought. It lands in your psoas, your diaphragm, the back of your throat where a scream has been lodged since 1988.

Pat Ogden, a pioneer in sensorimotor psychotherapy, describes this as a “procedural memory” held in the body’s posture and action patterns. Your child’s defiant scream is not just a sound—your nervous system reads it as a physical blow because it mirrors the pitch and intensity of threats long past. Ogden might invite you to notice where in your body the tension first arises when you hear that wail. For many parents, it is the hands. The hands want to grip, strike, silence. But if you trace that impulse backward, you find a child’s hands—your own—gripping a lunch table, a bicycle handlebar, a father’s belt, frozen in a moment where you needed protection and received none. Your body is not overreacting. It is re-enacting. The tantrum is a needle, and your nervous system is a scratch in an old record.

### Your Nervous System Has a Hair Trigger

Peter Levine’s work on the body’s threat response clarifies why your reaction feels so out of proportion. Trauma, in his model, is not stored as a narrative but as an incomplete biological cycle. When you were young and felt fear, your body prepared to fight or flee. If neither was safe nor possible, the energy got locked in your tissues, a frozen emergency light still spinning decades later. Now your child screams because you cut the sandwich wrong, and your body does not hear “sandwich.” It hears the distress frequency that, in your childhood, meant danger. Your heart rate spikes, your vision narrows, and you are suddenly not a parent but a cornered animal.

Here is a counterintuitive claim that will sting: Your child’s tantrum is not causing your rage. It is releasing it. The rage was already there, coiled in your perineum and jaw, waiting for a key. Levine uses the term “fixated arousal” for this stored activation. You might recognize it as the feeling of being perpetually on the edge of something—a tension in your ribs that never fully eases, a readiness to pounce or weep. When the tantrum comes, it does not create that pressure. It pokes a hole in the dam you built to contain it. The fury that floods you is your own unfelt terror, finally finding an outlet. So when you scream at your child to “just stop,” you are not really talking to them. You are talking to the noise you were never allowed to make.

### Counterintuitive: Anger Is the Guard Dog, Not the Intruder

Most parenting advice treats your anger as the intruder—a defect to banish. But Beverly Engel, a therapist who has spent decades mapping the interplay of shame and rage, offers a different view. In her work on emotionally abusive relationships and their intergenerational legacy, she describes anger as a secondary emotion that almost always serves as armor for something more vulnerable. When your child screeches that you are “the worst mommy ever,” the immediate flame in your chest is anger, but beneath it, so fast you miss it, is a shard of shame. Shame that you are failing. Shame that you are being seen failing. Shame that you, too, were once a child who needed to yell and got punished for it.

Engel calls this the “shame-rage cycle.” The child’s defiance triggers your old shame—perhaps the memory of being humiliated for a messy room or a loud voice. That shame is unbearable for more than a nanosecond, so your body converts it into rage, which feels powerful, righteous, even clean. Rage gets things done. Rage makes you tall when you feel small. But it’s a guard dog you set on the wrong threat. Your child is not the enemy. The enemy is the part of you that was never allowed to be a messy, loud, inconvenient child without paying for it with love. Until you can be tender with that part, you will keep deploying the dog.

### The Echo of Your Own Unheard Tantrums

Think back. Not to your parenting—to your childhood. Were you permitted a full, shaking, snotty meltdown? Or were you told to go to your room “until you can be pleasant”? Were you soothed, or were you ignored until you learned to swallow the storm? Most parents who rage during tantrums were once children whose tantrums were met with a wall. A face that turned away. A silence that lasted hours. A spoonful of soap on the tongue. So you learned to shut the tantrum down inside yourself, and now, when your child refuses to do the same, a primal panic grips you. Her uncontained emotion threatens to unlock the dungeon where you stuffed your own.

This is where the body memory becomes gruesomely specific. You might notice that your rage spikes not at the start of the tantrum, but when your child does the thing you were never allowed to do: scream directly at you, hit your leg, throw her body onto the floor. Your stomach knots. Your throat contracts as if a hand is pressing on it. That is the hand of your own mother, your own father, the invisible choreographer of your earliest nervous system coding. You are not angry at your child. You are terrified of what happens to children who lose control. And because you live inside your own skin, that terror translates instantly into the impulse to control—to grab, to threaten, to silence—because silence is what you mistook for safety.

### What Really Happens in Your Flesh When Your Child Screams

Let’s get granular. The next time your child starts to wind up, do not try to stop it or manage it. Just notice, by the hot crawl of sensation in your arms and the way your breath turns shallow and ragged at the top of your chest. Your trapezius muscles cinch upward toward your ears. A bitter metallic taste may flood your mouth as your sympathetic nervous system dumps adrenaline. Your inner thighs tighten, a reflex from a younger body trying to curl into a ball. Your auditory canal literally narrows, shifting your hearing to low-frequency threat detection, which is why your child’s high whine becomes unbearable—your body is primed to hear the growl of a predator, not the protest of a preschooler.

At the same time, a more secret sensation: a hollowing behind your sternum, a sort of internal collapse. That is your ventral vagal system, your social engagement system, going offline. You lose access to empathy, to perspective, to your own face softening. You become a threat response in human form. If you’ve ever said something cruel during a tantrum, something you instantly regretted, you know this place. It is not your “true self.” It is your pre-language self, the self that lived before you had words to narrate your pain. That self only knows sensation and survival. In that moment, you are not a bad parent. You are a body that has forgotten it is no longer in the original danger.

### A Five-Minute Practice to Hold Your Own Activation

Here is something you can do, not as a discipline tactic, but as a way to re-enter your body when the threat response has hijacked it. You will need no tools, only your own hands and the stubborn willingness to stay present.

First, the instant you register that hot surge, press both palms flat against a solid surface—a wall, a countertop, your own thighs if you’re seated. Push until you feel the muscles in your forearms engage. This gives your body the proprioceptive input it needs to locate itself in space, an ancient cue that you are not falling, not fleeing.

Second, with your palms still pressed, shift your gaze to something in the room that is not moving. A doorknob. A plug socket. A crumb on the floor. Focus on its stillness and let your eyes rest there for three full breaths. The visual system anchors the nervous system.

Third, and this is the hard one, feel the sensation in your throat. That tightness, that urge to yell—locate its exact edge. Take a sip of air over that edge, not into your cramped chest but behind the constriction, as if you are breathing around a stone. Imagine the exhale dropping down your spine into your pelvic floor. You are not trying to relax. You are trying to make room for the tension to exist without becoming it. Pat Ogden calls this “widening the container.” You are not silencing the rage. You are giving it a larger body to live in.

This practice will not stop your child’s tantrum. It will not make you a serene parent in five minutes. But if you do it consistently, you build a bridge of micro-actions between the old, automatic response and a new, chosen one. Each time you choose pressure over explosion, you lay a new neural pathway.

### The Partner Who Walks Away—And Why That Fuels Your Fire

If you have a partner, their reaction to the tantrum can feel like a second assault. Maybe they leave the room. Maybe they shoot you a look that says *control your kid*. Maybe they remain infuriatingly calm, which reads to your activated body as rejection, as abandonment. You are drowning in sound and adrenaline, and they are standing on the shore, arms crossed. You want to scream at them as much as at the child.

This dynamic often has a gender shape. The father who retreats, the mother who resents being left with the mess. But strip away the cultural script, and you find something rawer. Your partner’s withdrawal triggers your own childhood fear of being left alone with a volatile parent, or a depressed one, or a screaming one. You feel once again like a child who must manage an impossible emotional atmosphere all by yourself. The book [Host Defense Lion's Mane Powder](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07TJ63MH3?tag=spankyspinola-20) confronts this pattern in men directly, dissecting the way emotional shutdown in partners is often a fawn-freeze response dressed up as rationality. Masters insists that the one who walks away is not the “calm” one but the dysregulated one who performs control by removing themselves. If that is you—or your partner—naming it as dysregulation, not preference, changes something. You stop arguing about who is mature and start understanding that both bodies are drowning in different fluids.

### FAQ: The Questions You’re Too Ashamed to Ask

**Q: Does my rage mean I’m traumatizing my child the way I was traumatized?**
A: The terrifying truth is that you are not destined to repeat the exact actions, but you can transmit the same autonomic music—the unpredictable, charged atmosphere that teaches a child to scan adult faces for danger. The difference lies in repair. If you let your child see you reclaim yourself afterward, if you say “I was very big and scary, and you did not deserve that,” you rewire the memory for both of you. Trauma is not the rupture alone; it is the rupture without repair. Engel emphasizes this: shaming yourself for the outburst is a subtle way of making it about you again, when your child needs you to make it about their experience.

**Q: I don’t even feel angry—I feel numb or totally gone. Is that the same thing?**
A: Yes. Numbness is anger’s undercover cousin. If you dissociate during tantrums—if your vision goes flat, if you feel like you’re watching the scene from three feet behind your head—your nervous system has gone into freeze. Levine would describe this as a higher-threshold dorsal vagal response. Your rage didn’t vanish; it went underground, and it will resurface later as migraine, jaw pain, inexplicable exhaustion, or a sudden weep over spilled milk. Your body is still keeping score.

**Q: I’ve tried breathing exercises and they don’t work. They make me angrier. What’s wrong with me?**
A: Nothing is wrong with you. Standard breathing exercises fail because they ask a fight-or-flight body to do a relaxation response it does not trust. You cannot breathe your way out of a tiger attack. When you force shallow, calm breaths over a galloping heart, your nervous system reads it as suppression, another layer of the old “swallow your feelings” command. Before you breathe, you need to move. Shake your hands. Stomp your feet. Roll your shoulders with audible exhales. Give the activation a motor exit before you try to breathe it down. [Herb Pharm Stress Manager (1 oz)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005JW1F1C?tag=spankyspinola-20) offers a framework for actually metabolizing anger as energy, not demonizing it. She writes that anger needs a righteous job—setting a boundary, taking a physical action that asserts agency—before it will release its grip. If you skip that step, breathing is mockery.

### From Rupture to Repair: What Your Child Actually Needs

You may believe that your child needs you to be perfect. They don’t. They need you to be predictable in your repair. When you lose it and then come back, sit on the floor where they can see your face, and say “I hurt you, and I’m here now,” you teach your child that conflict does not equal annihilation. This does not require eloquent words. A rising five-year-old once told me that after his mother’s shout, she put her forehead to his and whispered, “My big voice scared us both. I’m going to get us some water.” That boy did not remember the shout. He remembered the forehead, the water.

Here’s where a quiet, biological ally can enter. Glycine, an amino acid, acts as an inhibitory neurotransmitter and can help cool a hyperaroused nervous system. If you find yourself circling into fight mode well after the tantrum ends—your thoughts looping, your muscles refusing to unclench—a slightly sweet-tasting powder like [Gaia Herbs Sound Sleep](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0036THNRA?tag=spankyspinola-20) taken with water may take the razor edge off your physiological arousal. I am not recommending a pill as a substitute for the deep, messy work. I am telling you that sometimes your body needs a chemical handbrake to let the deeper rewiring take hold.

### This Rage Is a Compass, Not a Crime

You have been told that your anger is a failure of love. It is not. It is a tattered map of everything you survived before you knew the word *survive*. Every time your child’s back arches in defiance, some child-sized version of you arches inside your chest, demanding to be seen, demanding to be held, demanding not to be silenced. You cannot parent from that part. But you can parent with it in the room. The difference is placing a hand on your abdomen when the heat comes and naming the ghost: *This is not only this moment. This is a long, old thing. I am here now. My skin is holding all of it.*

The practice is not to stop the rage. The practice is to stop pretending it isn’t yours. Your child’s tantrum is a shriek sent into the future from your own past. Can you hear it without crumbling? The body will speak whether you listen or not. You might as well lean in close. I write more about the body’s hidden language and the architecture of nervous system repair [at the Oracle Lover](https://theoraclelover.com)—where the assignment is not transcendence but the deep, local work of staying inside your own flesh long enough to know what it’s saying.

And when you do lean in, you might find that the rage is not a monster. It is a messenger in a monstrous mask, and its message is simpler than you think: *Someone needed to hold me. Someone needed to let me scream. No one did. Now it’s my turn, and I am terrified I am failing again.* That terror is not a sin. It is a door. Walk through it, and you will not find a placid parent on the other side, but you might find a real one—one who shakes and sweats and sometimes yells, and who keeps coming back to the child, again and again, not out of guilt, but out of a fierce, embodied commitment to break the chain in real time, one shriek at a time.
